ContactMessage.title: ContactMessage
ContactMessage.description: Represents a message sent by a user through the website's contact form. This entity facilitates the persistence of inquiries made via the 'Send a Message' section, which implies a backend processing capability not explicitly detailed in the initial UI-focused app requirements. This entity assumes the addition of a backend to handle form submissions and data storage.

id.description: Unique identifier for the ContactMessage entity.

fullName.description: The full name of the person sending the message.

email.description: The email address of the person sending the message.

subject.description: The subject of the inquiry, selected from predefined options (e.g., 'Registration Query', 'Paper Submission', 'Publication Info', 'Other').

message.description: The detailed content of the message sent by the user.

sentAt.description: The timestamp when the contact message was sent.

definition.description: Stores individual contact messages submitted through the website's contact form. Messages are created by any user (authenticated or unauthenticated) and are intended to be read by authorized administrators/conference organizers. No denormalized authorization fields are required within the document itself, as read authorization is based on a global role check for the requesting user.

params.name: contactMessageId
params.description: Unique identifier for a specific contact message.

firestore.reasoning: The recommended Firestore structure for the `ContactMessage` entity is a top-level collection named `/contact_messages`. Each document within this collection will represent a single contact message submitted via the website. This simple, flat structure is chosen because `ContactMessage` entities are independent submissions and do not inherently belong to a user's private data or a collaborative project. This design prioritizes direct access and simplifies security rules.Authorization Independence: This structure achieves authorization independence by storing the `ContactMessage` documents in a single, dedicated collection. Access to create these messages can be granted broadly (e.g., to any user, authenticated or unauthenticated), while read/list access would be restricted to specific authorized personnel (e.g., conference organizers or administrators). Critically, the authorization for reading/listing these messages does NOT depend on any attributes within the `ContactMessage` document itself, nor on any parent document. Instead, it relies on a separate, global role check (e.g., checking if `request.auth.uid` exists in an `/admin_roles` collection). This eliminates the need for `get()` calls in security rules to determine access, adhering to the core principle of Authorization Independence.QAPs (Rules are not Filters): The structure supports QAPs for list operations by applying authorization at the collection level. For instance, creating a message would be allowed for everyone, making it a simple `allow create: true` (or similar for authenticated/unauthenticated users). For reading and listing, the rule would check the requesting user's global role (e.g., `allow read, list: if exists(/databases/$(database)/documents/admin_roles/$(request.auth.uid));`). If the user is an administrator, they can perform a `list` query on `/contact_messages` and retrieve all documents. If they are not an administrator, the entire `list` operation will be denied upfront, preventing any data leakage or inefficient filtering after data retrieval. This ensures that security rules act as query enforcers, not post-query filters.
